worth_it_explanation: Geo targeting decides who is eligible to see an ad before any keyword, bid, or creative comes into play, so a mistake here wastes spend on people the campaign was never meant to reach. The location-targeting setting that controls presence versus interest sits in one campaign panel, and the location report shows where ads actually served against where they were meant to serve. Reading the two together takes a few minutes per campaign and surfaces spend leaving the service area, locations the business cannot fulfill, and a flat geographic bid that ignores where conversions happen. Each finding is a setting change rather than a rebuild, and the saved spend is available from the next click.
intro: Location targeting controls who is eligible to see a campaign's ads based on where they are or where they have shown interest. Three settings drive most of the waste in this area. The presence-versus-interest option decides whether ads reach only people in the targeted area or also people who have shown interest in it, location bid adjustments decide how much to bid by geography, and the list of targeted locations decides where the campaign competes at all. These checks confirm that each setting matches where the business can actually serve customers and earn conversions.

audit_checks.title: Campaigns use 'Presence or interest' targeting where 'Presence' is intended
audit_checks.impact_rationale: The 'Presence or interest' setting makes ads eligible for users outside the targeted area who have only shown interest in it. For a business that can serve customers only in a defined area, that eligibility spends budget on clicks that cannot convert, and the spend is hard to see because the location report still lists the targeted area as the target.

educate.base:
Google Ads gives each campaign a location-targeting option that decides who counts as inside a targeted area. The default is 'Presence or interest', which makes ads eligible for people who are in or regularly in the targeted location and also for people who have shown interest in it, such as searching for it or viewing content about it. The alternative, 'Presence', limits eligibility to people who are in or regularly in the location.

The default fits a business that wants to reach people researching a destination from elsewhere, such as a hotel or a travel service. It works against a business that can only serve a local area because a user searching from another state for a city the business operates in becomes eligible for the ad, clicks, and cannot convert. The targeted location reads as correct in the campaign settings, so the spend on out-of-area interest traffic is hard to see without opening the location report and comparing matched locations to targeted locations.

Google describes this control under advanced location options and notes the two settings change who is eligible, not just who is reported. Switching travel, real estate, and education advertisers from 'Presence' to 'Presence or interest' raised conversions for them, per Google's own guidance on advanced location options, which is the same reason the reverse setting matters for a local-only business, where interest traffic adds cost without conversions.

A few signs suggest a campaign is set wider than the business can serve:
  • The campaign serves a local or regional business but uses the default 'Presence or interest' option
  • The location report shows matched locations outside the targeted area
  • Clicks arrive from regions the business has no way to fulfill
You may want to confirm the targeting option against what the business can actually serve before deciding the targeted locations alone are correct. The locations can be right while the eligibility setting is still too broad.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: A store that ships nationally may be fine on the default option since interest traffic from anywhere in the shipping area can still convert. A store with regional fulfillment, local pickup, or shipping limits is a different case. There, 'Presence or interest' makes ads eligible for shoppers outside the fulfillment area who searched for the region, and those clicks rarely end in an order the store can fill. You may want to match the option to the actual delivery footprint rather than the broader brand interest.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: A lead-generation campaign for a service business, such as a contractor or a clinic, usually wants people who are in the service area, not people researching it from elsewhere. On the default option, a form fill from outside the service area is a cost with no serviceable lead behind it. You may want to set the option to 'Presence' for service-area campaigns and reserve 'Presence or interest' for cases where remote interest is a genuine prospect.

investigate.intro: Confirm each campaign's location-targeting option and compare the location report's matched locations against the targeted area to see where ads actually served.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open a campaign, then 'Settings', and expand the 'Locations' section. Open the advanced location options and note whether the campaign uses 'Presence or interest' or 'Presence'.
Compare that setting against what the business can serve. A local or regional business on 'Presence or interest' is the case to flag.

Open the campaign's location report and switch from the targeted-locations view to the matched-locations view. Note any matched locations that fall outside the area the business can serve, which are the clicks the interest setting is allowing in.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per campaign, the location-targeting option, the area the business can serve, and whether the matched-locations report shows serving outside that area. A campaign whose option matches its serviceable area, with no meaningful out-of-area matched spend, passes. A local or regional campaign on 'Presence or interest' with matched locations outside the serviceable area is a fail recorded with the campaign name and the out-of-area locations observed.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the location-targeting option and the matched-locations observation for each campaign above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more of {{site_name}}'s campaigns use the 'Presence or interest' location option in cases where the business can only serve a defined area. That setting makes ads eligible for people outside the area who have only shown interest in it, so budget reaches clicks that cannot convert while the targeted location still reads as correct. {{voice_recommend}} setting the location option to 'Presence' on campaigns that serve a fixed area, and reserving 'Presence or interest' for campaigns where remote interest is a genuine prospect. After the change, review the matched-locations report to confirm serving has pulled back to the serviceable area.

audit_checks.title: Campaigns apply no location bid adjustments despite uneven geographic performance
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A campaign with a single flat bid across every targeted location spends the same to compete in areas that convert well and areas that convert poorly. Where performance varies by geography, that flat bid overpays in weak areas and underbids in strong ones, which holds back efficiency that a location adjustment could recover. The effect is a steady drag rather than a hard break, which is why it sits below the targeting-eligibility issues.

educate.base:
A location bid adjustment raises or lowers a campaign's bid for users in a specific country, region, city, or radius. Google supports adjustments from -90% to +900% at the campaign level, and when adjustments overlap, the most specific one applies, so a city adjustment overrides a country adjustment for that city.

Manual bid adjustments by location apply to campaigns that set their own bids. A campaign on a Smart Bidding strategy, such as Target cost per acquisition (CPA) or Target return on ad spend (ROAS), already factors location into its automated bids, so location adjustments are limited or ignored there and the right lever is the bid target rather than a manual adjustment. The check below is about manual-bidding campaigns that target several locations with measurably different results and still bid the same everywhere.

The location report is where the difference shows. It breaks results out by geography, so a region with a strong conversion rate and a region with a weak one are visible side by side. When that spread is wide and the campaign sets its own bids with no location adjustments in place, the bid is leaving money on the table in the strong region and spending too freely in the weak one.

A few signs suggest location bid adjustments are worth adding:
  • The campaign uses manual or enhanced cost-per-click bidding rather than a Smart Bidding strategy
  • The location report shows a wide spread in conversion rate or cost per conversion across targeted regions
  • No location bid adjustments are set, so every region competes at the same bid
You may want to base any adjustment on enough conversions to trust the pattern rather than on a few clicks. A thin sample can point the bid the wrong way.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: A store often sees geographic spread driven by shipping cost, delivery time, or regional demand for a product line. The location report can show which regions return the best revenue against spend. On a manual-bidding campaign, you may want to lift bids where return on ad spend is strong and trim them where it is weak, rather than holding one bid across the whole country.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: A service business with multiple locations or service areas often converts at very different rates by region, driven by competition and demand. You may want to read the location report by service area and set adjustments that favor the areas filling pipeline, while easing off areas that draw clicks but few qualified leads.

investigate.intro: Identify manual-bidding campaigns that target several locations, read their geographic performance, and check whether any location bid adjustments are in place.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open a campaign and note its bid strategy. A Smart Bidding strategy handles location in its automated bids, so focus on campaigns using manual or enhanced cost-per-click.

Open the campaign's location report and read conversion rate and cost per conversion by region. Note any region with enough conversions to trust the pattern that performs well above or below the campaign average.
Open the locations settings and check whether any location bid adjustments are set. A campaign with a wide performance spread and no adjustments is the case to flag.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per manual-bidding campaign, whether the location report shows a meaningful performance spread across targeted regions and whether any location bid adjustments are in place. A campaign with even geographic performance, or with adjustments that already reflect the spread, passes. A campaign with a wide, well-evidenced spread and no location bid adjustments is a fail recorded with the campaign name and the regions that diverge most.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the bid strategy, the geographic performance spread, and the presence of location bid adjustments for each campaign above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more of {{site_name}}'s manual-bidding campaigns bid the same across every targeted location while the location report shows geographic performance varying widely. A flat bid overpays in regions that convert poorly and underbids in regions that convert well, so efficiency that a location adjustment could recover is left on the table. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing geographic performance per campaign and setting location bid adjustments that lift bids in strong regions and reduce them in weak ones, based on enough conversions to trust the pattern. After the change, watch the location report to confirm the adjustments move spend toward the better-performing areas.

audit_checks.title: Campaigns target locations the business cannot serve
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Targeting a country, region, or radius the business cannot ship to, deliver to, or service spends budget on clicks that have no path to a conversion. Unlike the presence-versus-interest setting, this is the targeted location list itself being wrong, so the waste is structural and continues every day the location stays in the list.

educate.base:
A campaign's targeted locations decide where it is eligible to compete at all. Google lets a campaign target and exclude countries, regions, cities, postal codes, and radius areas, with up to 1,000 location targets in a single campaign. The list is only correct when every targeted location is a place the business can actually serve.

A targeted location the business cannot fulfill spends budget on clicks that cannot convert. The causes are usually practical rather than strategic. A radius drawn loosely around a storefront pulls in an adjacent area the business does not cover, a country is targeted out of optimism while shipping or service is regional, or an old expansion plan left locations in the list after the plan changed. The campaign keeps competing in those places because the location list still names them, even though no order or booking can come from them.

The two views of the location report tell the story together. The targeted-locations view lists what the campaign meant to target, and the matched-locations view shows where ads served. Reading them against the real service or fulfillment footprint surfaces the targeted entries that should be removed or replaced with exclusions.

A few signs suggest the targeted location list has drifted from what the business can serve:
  • The list includes regions, countries, or radius areas outside the documented service or shipping footprint
  • The matched-locations report shows conversions concentrated in a subset of the targeted area while spend is spread across all of it
  • Old or speculative locations remain in the list after the plan behind them changed
You may want to confirm the targeted locations against where the business can take and fulfill an order before trimming, so a productive area is not removed by mistake.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: A store's serviceable area is its shipping footprint. You may want to compare the targeted countries and regions against where the store actually ships, and remove or exclude any targeted location the cart will not accept an address for. A radius around a physical store should match the area customers realistically travel from, not a wide circle that includes towns the store rarely sees.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: A service business serves a defined set of areas, set by where its teams travel or where it holds the licenses to operate. You may want to align the targeted locations to that documented service area, and exclude neighboring regions that generate form fills the business cannot act on. A lead from outside the service area is a cost with no serviceable prospect behind it.

investigate.intro: Compare each campaign's targeted locations against the documented service or shipping footprint, and use the location report to see where spend and conversions actually land.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open a campaign, then 'Settings', and expand the 'Locations' section to list every targeted location and any exclusions.
Compare that list against where the business can ship, deliver, or service. Note any targeted country, region, or radius that falls outside the serviceable footprint.

Open the location report in both the targeted-locations and matched-locations views. Note any targeted area drawing spend with few or no conversions, which points to a location that belongs out of the list or under an exclusion.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per campaign, the targeted locations, the business's serviceable footprint, and whether the location report shows spend in areas the business cannot serve. A campaign whose targeted locations all fall within the serviceable footprint passes. A campaign targeting one or more locations outside that footprint is a fail recorded with the campaign name and the locations that should be removed or excluded.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the targeted locations and the serviceable footprint for each campaign above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more of {{site_name}}'s campaigns target locations the business cannot serve, such as regions outside the shipping or service footprint. Budget competes for clicks in those places with no path to a conversion, and the waste continues every day the location stays in the list. {{voice_recommend}} aligning each campaign's targeted locations to the documented serviceable area, removing or excluding locations outside it, and confirming the change against the matched-locations report. After the change, review the location report to confirm spend has concentrated in the areas the business can fulfill.
